customer_anxiety_profile: Jennifer is not anxious — she is low-urgency. She booked willingly but framed the appointment as low-commitment browsing ('think about it for a month or two'). She is curious about ROI framing (cost-in vs. value-back) and skeptical of government incentives. No hostility, no scam concern, no financial distress signals.
unresolved_concerns: Jennifer's stated intent to defer any decision for 'a month or two' — she has not been given a reason to treat this appointment as time-sensitive.
Government incentive skepticism — she explicitly said she is not counting on them; the specialist should address what the numbers look like without incentives.
Jennifer has only two months of bills at this address and does not yet know her full annual usage pattern — the specialist should confirm the square-footage-based design approach Carter mentioned.
sensitive_topics_raised: Foot surgery — Jennifer has been home for six weeks recovering; she is available but may be physically limited.
Recent move — Jennifer has been at this address approximately ten weeks; she is still learning the home's utility patterns.
exact_promises_rep_made: A specialist (not Carter) will be on the call.
Carter will send a text with the appointment details.
The appointment will take approximately thirty to forty minutes.
The specialist will have 'new programs' to go over — Carter's phrasing was vague; do not over-promise on specific incentive programs.
closer_should_know: Jennifer booked easily but is in browsing mode — she said 'think about it for a month or two.' She has not been anchored to her bill pain or given a reason to treat this as a decision conversation. Her own words ('what kind of investment you have to put in initially and what type you get back later') show she is ROI-oriented. Lead with her specific design numbers and the cost-in vs. value-back framing she articulated herself.
